name: Technical Demonstrator Architecture
lever_classification: methodology — technical architecture for fallback authentication models
consequences: Prioritizing FIDO2/WebAuthn-based authentication accelerates development and aligns with open standards, but risks underrepresenting hardware-token alternatives that could offer stronger isolation from OS-level vulnerabilities. This choice may limit the scope of fallback options presented to policymakers.
options: Design demonstrators around FIDO2/WebAuthn with GrapheneOS to emphasize open-standard compliance and privacy-respecting execution environments
Develop a hardware-token-based fallback path using open-hardware specifications to create an OS-agnostic authentication layer
Build a browser-first prototype leveraging WebAuthn and decentralized identifiers to minimize reliance on mobile OS ecosystems
review: Focusing on technical demonstrators risks overemphasizing feasibility at the expense of political viability; none of the options adequately address how to counter convenience-driven arguments from platform vendors.

name: Stakeholder Engagement Strategy
lever_classification: execution — coalition-building priorities for policy influence
consequences: Prioritizing civil-society coalitions strengthens democratic accountability but may dilute technical credibility with institutional stakeholders. This approach could delay formal engagement with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen until after critical policy windows have closed.
options: Anchor advocacy in privacy-advocate networks to frame digital sovereignty as a civic imperative
Target academic researchers and security experts to generate technical credibility for policy proposals
Leverage Folketinget committee members to create legislative momentum for procurement reforms
review: The options fail to address how to engage platform vendors constructively; all pathways assume adversarial relationships rather than potential collaboration on fallback mechanisms.

name: Regulatory Framing Approach
lever_classification: governance — policy narrative for institutional adoption
consequences: Focusing on supplier concentration risk increases chances of institutional buy-in but may marginalize privacy concerns that could strengthen long-term resilience. This framing could limit the scope of required fallback capabilities to mere compliance checkboxes.
options: Position platform neutrality as a critical-resilience requirement under NSIS 'Substantial' standards
Frame fallback access as a democratic accountability issue requiring public-sector transparency
Link supplier concentration risks to national cybersecurity strategy frameworks and threat assessments
review: The options overlook how to reconcile resilience requirements with existing eIDAS2 compliance expectations, creating potential conflicts with EU interoperability mandates.

name: Procurement Influence Mechanism
lever_classification: execution — institutional procurement strategy
consequences: Targeting AltID procurement processes creates direct leverage but risks being sidelined by technical complexities in the wallet rollout. This approach may force the project to compromise on core platform-neutrality goals to secure incremental wins.
options: Embed platform-neutrality requirements in AltID's technical specifications through formal stakeholder consultations
Leverage EU Digital Identity Wallet working groups to align Danish procurement language with broader interoperability standards
Develop procurement templates that explicitly require fallback authentication pathways in public-sector RFPs
review: None of the options address how to counteract the natural inertia of public-sector procurement cycles, which often favor established platform integrations.

name: Risk Mitigation Framework
lever_classification: operations — risk assessment methodology for dependency analysis
consequences: Prioritizing technical risk assessments strengthens credibility but may delay policy engagement. This approach could create a gap between technical evidence and institutional readiness to act on the findings.
options: Conduct threat modeling focused on platform-policy changes and geopolitical disruptions to public-sector authentication
Map dependency surfaces across MitID's app-distribution and device-integrity workflows to identify critical failure points
Develop scenario analyses for alternative authentication pathways under varying regulatory and technical constraints
review: The options assume technical risks are the primary barrier, neglecting the political and organizational resistance that may block even well-supported solutions.

name: Policy Narrative Framing
lever_classification: dissemination — messaging strategy for institutional audiences
consequences: Emphasizing continuity-of-service risks increases chances of institutional attention but may downplay long-term sovereignty benefits. This framing could lead to short-term fixes that perpetuate underlying dependencies.
options: Position platform-neutral access as a critical-resilience requirement for public-sector digital services
Link fallback authentication to national cybersecurity strategy frameworks and threat assessments
Frame supplier concentration as a threat to democratic governance and public-service reliability
review: The options fail to address how to counter the perception that platform-neutral solutions are inherently less user-friendly or less secure than existing integrations.

name: Interoperability Standards Strategy
lever_classification: methodology — which open standards to prioritize for fallback authentication
consequences: Selecting specific open standards for fallback authentication accelerates technical credibility but locks in compatibility with existing infrastructure. A narrow focus on FIDO2 risks replicating dependency patterns under a different label, while a modular approach demands ongoing governance overhead.
options: Prioritize FIDO2/WebAuthn for browser-based fallback
Develop a hybrid model integrating OpenID Connect with proprietary APIs
Adopt a modular standards framework allowing protocol swaps
review: Prioritizing FIDO2 ensures compatibility but risks entrenching another proprietary standard. The options overlook potential EU regulatory shifts toward more open protocols.

name: Procurement Clause Negotiation
lever_classification: execution — how to structure procurement requirements for platform neutrality
consequences: Embedding platform-neutral requirements in procurement contracts forces institutional compliance but faces resistance from vendors. Early engagement with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen could secure pilot exemptions but risks dilution of core demands.
options: Lobby for mandatory fallback-path clauses in all public-sector identity contracts
Create a tiered procurement framework with platform-neutral options
Secure exemptions for pilot implementations in non-critical services
review: Mandatory clauses create legal pressure but ignore vendor lobbying capabilities. The options underestimate the complexity of renegotiating existing procurement frameworks.

name: Policy Alignment Mechanism
lever_classification: governance — how to frame digital sovereignty within EU regulatory constraints
consequences: Reframing dependency risks as resilience imperatives shifts policy discourse but requires sustained advocacy. Aligning with eIDAS2 standards strengthens technical legitimacy but may compromise sovereignty goals.
options: Anchor proposals in EU resilience mandates rather than national sovereignty rhetoric
Propose dual-track certification for platform-dependent and platform-neutral systems
Leverage Section 106 heritage-like protections for digital infrastructure continuity
review: EU alignment enhances credibility but dilutes national control. The options fail to address how to reconcile conflicting regulatory priorities.

name: Decentralized Trust Framework
lever_classification: methodology — alternative approaches to device and app integrity verification
consequences: Building a trust model independent of platform integrity signals increases security but requires redefining authentication metrics. Community-driven validation mechanisms face adoption hurdles without institutional backing.
options: Implement blockchain-based attestation for device integrity
Create a federated trust network with academic and civil-society validators
Develop hardware-token-centric authentication with open-source firmware
review: Blockchain solutions introduce new complexity without proven scalability. The options neglect the political challenges of displacing established trust anchors.

name: EU Regulatory Advocacy
lever_classification: dissemination — how to shape European digital identity policy
consequences: Influencing European Digital Identity Wallet standards creates cross-border leverage but requires navigating bureaucratic inertia. Early engagement with technical working groups builds credibility but risks co-optation by incumbent interests.
options: Propose mandatory fallback-path requirements in EU-wide identity wallet specifications
Form a Nordic coalition to standardize platform-neutral authentication across member states
Leverage GDPR enforcement mechanisms to mandate interoperability
review: EU advocacy depends on external political will rather than technical merits. The options underestimate the influence of tech industry lobbying in Brussels.

name: Stakeholder Coalition Dynamics
lever_classification: execution — managing coalition structure and decision-making
consequences: Building a broad coalition strengthens political pressure but introduces coordination challenges. Aligning privacy advocates with procurement officials requires balancing conflicting priorities.
options: Create a multi-stakeholder council with voting rights proportional to institutional influence
Develop a tiered engagement model with core allies and peripheral supporters
Use adversarial simulations to test coalition resilience against vendor counterattacks
review: Tiered engagement risks fragmenting collective action. The options ignore the potential for internal ideological conflicts within the coalition.

name: Fallback Infrastructure Design
lever_classification: product — architectural choices for platform-neutral authentication infrastructure
consequences: Prototyping a standalone fallback system increases technical credibility but diverts resources from policy work. Minimalist designs risk underestimating real-world deployment complexities.
options: Build a minimal viable fallback system with limited service scope
Design a modular infrastructure compatible with existing identity providers
Create a simulated fallback environment for regulatory demonstrations
review: Minimalist designs lack operational realism. The options fail to address how to transition from simulation to actual deployment.

name: Open Authentication Protocol Integration
lever_classification: methodology — adoption of open authentication protocols
consequences: Adopting open authentication protocols reduces reliance on proprietary platform signals, but requires ongoing maintenance to align with evolving technical standards. This shifts development burden to non-commercial entities, potentially creating gaps in real-world usability testing.
options: Implement FIDO2-based authentication with decentralized identity verification nodes
Develop a hybrid model combining open standards with legacy platform compatibility layers
Integrate with blockchain-anchored trust frameworks to bypass centralized OS mediation
review: Balancing open protocols against platform-specific security guarantees creates friction; none of the options address potential interoperability delays with existing MitID infrastructure.

name: Public-Private Partnership Governance
lever_classification: governance — structure of public-private collaboration
consequences: Establishing joint oversight bodies increases accountability for fallback systems but risks dilution of technical priorities through bureaucratic negotiation. This could slow innovation cycles without clear institutional mandates.
options: Create a cross-sector review panel with platform vendor and public-sector representatives
Deploy a phased collaboration model with incremental technical disclosure requirements
Form an independent technical audit commission with authority over procurement decisions
review: Core tension between public control and private innovation; options fail to account for vendor lobbying against non-discriminatory standards.

name: Cross-Platform Compliance Advocacy
lever_classification: dissemination — regulatory compliance strategy alignment
consequences: Engaging EU compliance bodies strengthens regulatory pressure on platform neutrality but may divert focus from national procurement priorities. This risks alignment with broader European frameworks at the expense of Danish-specific needs.
options: Leverage eIDAS2 compliance audits to mandate fallback authentication requirements
Align demonstrator architecture with ISO/IEC 27018 cloud compliance standards
Use national security certification processes to block platform-exclusive procurement contracts
review: Tension between EU alignment and national specificity; options overlook potential conflicts with existing eIDAS2 implementation timelines.

name: Procurement Mandate Strengthening
lever_classification: execution — procurement contract design
consequences: Embedding platform-neutrality clauses in procurement contracts forces institutional adoption of fallback mechanisms but requires sustained political capital to enforce. This could trigger pushback from vendors reliant on exclusive distribution channels.
options: Require multi-platform authentication support in all public-sector identity contracts
Develop a certification benchmark for non-proprietary identity solutions
Use competitive bidding processes to penalize platform-exclusive technical proposals
review: Balancing contractual mandates with vendor capabilities creates friction; options miss the risk of delayed implementation due to compliance complexity.

name: Security Certification Diversification
lever_classification: product — security validation strategy
consequences: Seeking certifications from non-traditional bodies enhances credibility for fallback systems but fragments validation processes across jurisdictions. This increases administrative overhead without guaranteed adoption.
options: Pursue NSIS compliance through alternative certification pathways
Develop a modular security framework accepted by multiple European regulatory authorities
Partner with non-EU security institutions to validate platform-neutral architectures
review: Tension between broad validation and regulatory specificity; options ignore potential duplication of certification efforts across agencies.

name: Stakeholder Influence Prioritization
lever_classification: execution — stakeholder engagement focus
consequences: Targeting specific legislative or regulatory bodies accelerates policy adoption but may exclude broader technical or civil-society input. This creates siloed advocacy efforts with limited systemic impact.
options: Focus engagement on Folketinget's digital policy committee for legislative backing
Target regional public administration leaders to pilot fallback mechanisms
Engage tech-savvy civil society groups to pressure platform vendors directly
review: Balancing centralized policy influence with decentralized advocacy creates tension; options underestimate grassroots resistance to technical complexity.

name: Resilience Testing Framework Development
lever_classification: operations — system resilience validation methodology
consequences: Creating scenario-based resilience tests exposes system vulnerabilities but requires significant resource allocation for simulation environments. This could delay demonstrator delivery if not tightly scoped.
options: Design failure-mode simulations for OS integrity signal disruptions
Implement continuous stress-testing against geopolitical supply-chain scenarios
Develop a third-party audit protocol for authentication path redundancy
review: Tension between comprehensive testing and time constraints; options omit resource allocation challenges for sustained testing cycles.
